preFix.title: Building Regulations Part L notification for retrofit insulation
preFix.subtitle: What triggers Building Control involvement and what does not
preFix.excerpt: Most domestic insulation work does not require Building Regulations approval. A handful of work types do: insulating more than 25% of a thermal element in some notifiable situations, and installing certain controlled services. This explains the notifiable boundary and what to do when a project crosses it.
preFix.sourceNotes: Building Regulations Approved Document L1B (England, 2022 edition). Planning Portal guidance on controlled work. LABC technical guidance notes on thermal elements. AECB competent person scheme for EWI contractors.

content.text: Most domestic insulation retrofits do not need Building Regulations approval. Loft insulation, draught-stripping, secondary glazing, and floor insulation are all exempt. The boundary shifts when a project is a 'major renovation' or involves notifiable work.
Part L
1B covers fuel and power conservation in existing homes and sets the thresholds.
Work that does NOT require notification
Adding loft insulation on top of what is there already. No structural work needed.
Draught-stripping doors, windows, skirting boards, floor gaps, and loft hatches.
Secondary glazing added to existing windows (the window frame itself is unchanged).
Pipe and cylinder lagging.
Fitting insulation below suspended timber floors (fitting does not change the floor structure).
Work that DOES require notification
The main notifiable trigger under Part L1B: renovating more than 50% of a thermal element (roof, floor, or wall) at once. The element must then meet the Part L1B target
U-value
if the improvement is feasible and pays back within 15 years.
Replacing more than 50% of the flat roof covering: the new work is notifiable under Part L1B, and the roof U-value must meet 0.18 W/m²·K or better.
Replacing a complete ground floor: if the whole floor is lifted and relaid, it is notifiable and must meet 0.22 W/m²·K.

External wall cladding replacement on the whole wall: if all render or cladding is being replaced, Part L1B requires insulation to 0.30 W/m²·K or better. This applies where the improvement is cost-effective (payback under 15 years).
Window and door replacements: notifiable as 'controlled fittings' and must meet U-value targets for the glass and whole window. Self-certified by FENSA or Certass.
Boiler replacement: notifiable, self-certified by Gas Safe. The new boiler must have a SEDBUK rating of 92% or better for gas.
Worked example: Victorian terrace

An owner of an 1890 terraced house in Bristol plans three projects: (1) strip and re-tile the entire rear slope of the existing pitched roof, (2) install EWI on the rear elevation, (3) add loft insulation top-up. Projects (1) and (2) are notifiable because more than 50% of a thermal element is being worked on. Project (3) is not notifiable. For (1), the contractor self-certifies the new flat-roofed rafter insulation meets 0.18 W/m²·K; for (2), an EWI contractor registered with BBA/KIWA certifies the system and submits to Building Control.
Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland

Scotland has its own building standards (Section 6, Energy). The notifiable thresholds are broadly similar to England but the U-value targets differ slightly. Wales uses the same approved documents as England. Northern Ireland has its own Technical Booklet F1. The principle is the same in all four: modest DIY insulation is exempt; major renovation of a thermal element is notifiable and must achieve the element-specific U-value target.
The EPC and notification
Notifiable work that improves a thermal element should be reflected in an updated
EPC
. After major retrofit, get a new EPC from a DEA assessor once the work is done. Give the Building Control completion certificate to the assessor for reference.
When to call a professional
Contractors on Gas Safe, FENSA, and NAPIT schemes can self-certify. For work outside these schemes, apply to Building Control before starting.
